coredata.dc:title: Nitrogen and sulphur co-doped multiwalled carbon nanotubes as an efficient electrocatalyst for improved oxygen electroreduction
coredata.prism:issueName: 4th International Conference on Nanoscience and Nanotechnology
coredata.dc:creator: Patil, Indrajit M.
Reddy, Vinusha
Lokanathan, Moorthi
Kakade, Bhalchandra
coredata.dc:description: The electroreduction of oxygen requires platinum (Pt)-based catalyst; however, Pt is extremely expensive and needs a systematic design of a cost effective electrocatalyst, without sacrificing its catalytic activity. Herein, firstly we synthesized nitrogen (-N) and sulphur (-S) co-doped carbon based quantum dots (NSCDs), which were then self-assembled on exfoliated multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) to prepare N- and S- co-doped multiwalled carbon nanotubes (NSCD/C) in the final step. Morphological study reveals a surface defects on CNT sidewalls and it has been further confirmed by Raman analysis. The elemental composition and its chemical environment were confirmed through energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) respectively. Importantly, the electrochemical results show interesting oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) properties of NSCD/C-600 catalyst (N- & S- co-doped MWCNTs annealed at 600 °C) with an onset potential (Eonset) of 0.87 V vs RHE and a substantial limiting current density (JL) of 5.0 mA cm−2 at 1600 rpm. Additionally, prepared catalyst shows a single step nearly 4-electron transfer pathway, signifying first order kinetics similar to that of Pt based catalyst. Further studies show that the catalyst exhibiting much better stability and higher resistance to methanol oxidation as compared to commercial Pt/C catalyst. The enhanced electrochemical activity can be attributed due to the presence of more defect sites in the carbon nanotubes as well as synergistic effect between both -N and -S co-doping in exfoliated CNTs, further leading to increase in both active doping sites and conductivity. Hence, this report provides scope to replace precious noble metal based catalysts with metal-free catalysts such as NSCD/C-600 in cathode compartment for low-temperature fuel cells (LTFCs) applications under alkaline condition.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon nanotube
Electrocatalyst
Oxygen reduction reaction
Fuel cells

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids confeditor contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb vol volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst pubtype ref specialabst

xocs:title-editors-group.ce:title: 4th International Conference on Nanoscience and Nanotechnology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NSCD)
Preparation of nitrogen & sulphur co-doped MWCNTs (NSCD/C)

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Department of Science and Technology Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB; No. SB/FT/CS-120/2012), India for financial support through the Young Scientist Fast track scheme. Authors also acknowledge SRM-IST for providing research infrastructure.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of nitrogen (N) & sulphur (S) based carbon quantum dots (step 1); and synthesis of N- and S- co-doped CNTs (step 2). Here, hydrogen atoms are hidden for more clarity; the grey, red, blue and yellow coloured spheres refer to C, O, N and S atoms respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) Comparative UV–vis absorption spectra for citric acid, L-cysteine and NSCDs. Inset shows, an digital photograph of the obtained NSCDs under illumination of white light (left) and UV light (right); (b) photoluminescence spectra of as-prepared NSCDs; (c) HRTEM image of NSCD/C-600 sample, where the red arrow indicates the surface defects due to sonochemical treatment; and (d) EDS spectrum of NSCD/C-600 and its elemental distribution (as shown in inset).
(a) Comparative powder XRD pattern of various NSCD/C based samples and fCNTs; and (b) comparative Raman spectra of NSCD/C-600 catalyst and fCNTs.
Deconvoluted spectra of (a) C 1s; (b) O 1s; (c) N 1s and (d) S 2p core level for NSCD/C-600 sample, whereas black and red lines indicate raw data and fitting curves respectively.

(a) CVs comparison of NSCD/C-600 catalyst, recorded in N2 and O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at a sweep rate of 20 mV s−1; (b) ORR polarization curves of NSCD/C-600 electrocatalyst under O2-saturated electrolyte with a sweep rates of 10 mV s−1; (c) Koutecky-Levich plot obtained at various potentials for NSCD/C-600; and (d) the percentage of peroxide production and number of electrons transferred (n) for NSCD/C-600 catalyst at a potential range of 0–0.8 V vs RHE, whereas inset shows RRDE voltammogram of NSCD/C-600 catalyst (here disc is scanned at a rate of 10 mV s−1 and the ring potential was kept constant at 1.5 V vs RHE).

(a) Comparative ORR polarization curves of all NSCD/C based catalysts at 1600 rpm under O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH; (b) Tafel plots of ORR polarization curves obtained from Fig. 4(a), whereas symbol (a), (b), (c), (d), (e) and (f) represents NSCD/C-600, NSCD/C-750, NSCD/C, NSCD/C-750*, NSCD/C-500 and NSCD/C-400 catalyst respectively; (c) mass activity for different NSCD/C based catalyst, calculated at 0.6 V versus RHE; and (d) histogram of comparative mass activity calculated at 0.6 V and 0.9 V for NSCD/C-600 and Pt/C catalysts respectively and geometric current density at 1600 rpm under similar experimental condition.

(a) ORR polarization curves of NSCD/C-600 catalyst, before and after 5k durable cycles under O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH recorded at scan rate 10 mV s−1; and (b) comparative chronoamperometric i-t (current versus time) response of NSCD/C-600 and Pt/C catalyst; whereas inset shows, active site poisoning response for NSCD/C-600 and Pt/C catalyst after addition of 1 M methanol in O2 saturated 0.1 M KOH solution at constant half wave potential.

head.ce:title: Nitrogen and sulphur co-doped multiwalled carbon nanotubes as an efficient electrocatalyst for improved oxygen electroreduction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: SRM Research Institute, SRM Institute of Science & Technology, Kattankulathur, 603 203 Chennai, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, SRM Institute of Science & Technology, Kattankulathur, 603 203 Chennai, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Nanotechnology, SRM Institute of Science & Technology, Kattankulathur, 603 203 Chennai, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: SRM Institute of Science & Technology, Kattankulathur, SRM Research Institute, Kattankulathur, Chennai, 603 203, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: An surface defective CNTs co-doped with -N and -S to enhance oxygen electroreduction kinetics.

The electroreduction of oxygen requires platinum (Pt)-based catalyst; however, Pt is extremely expensive and needs a systematic design of a cost effective electrocatalyst, without sacrificing its catalytic activity. Herein, firstly we synthesized nitrogen (-N) and sulphur (-S) co-doped carbon based quantum dots (NSCDs), which were then self-assembled on exfoliated multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) to prepare N- and S- co-doped multiwalled carbon nanotubes (NSCD/C) in the final step. Morphological study reveals a surface defects on CNT sidewalls and it has been further confirmed by Raman analysis. The elemental composition and its chemical environment were confirmed through energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) respectively. Importantly, the electrochemical results show interesting oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) properties of NSCD/C-600 catalyst (N- & S- co-doped MWCNTs annealed at 600 °C) with an onset potential (Eonset) of 0.87 V vs RHE and a substantial limiting current density (JL) of 5.0 mA cm−2 at 1600 rpm. Additionally, prepared catalyst shows a single step nearly 4-electron transfer pathway, signifying first order kinetics similar to that of Pt based catalyst. Further studies show that the catalyst exhibiting much better stability and higher resistance to methanol oxidation as compared to commercial Pt/C catalyst. The enhanced electrochemical activity can be attributed due to the presence of more defect sites in the carbon nanotubes as well as synergistic effect between both -N and -S co-doping in exfoliated CNTs, further leading to increase in both active doping sites and conductivity. Hence, this report provides scope to replace precious noble metal based catalysts with metal-free catalysts such as NSCD/C-600 in cathode compartment for low-temperature fuel cells (LTFCs) applications under alkaline condition.

ce:section.ce:para: In the electrochemical energy conversion device, such as proton exchange membrane fuel cells (PEMFCs), the reaction at cathode namely oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) has sluggish reaction kinetics compared to hydrogen oxidation reaction (HOR) at anode. The ORR kinetics has been maintained using platinum supported on carbon (Pt/C) as a state-of-the-art catalyst; however Pt being a noble metal and a scarce resource, as a result technology becomes more expensive for commercialization [1–3]. In recent studies, carbon materials including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), porous carbon materials, 2D-graphene and their derivatives are shown to possess excellent electric and thermal conductivity, and unique physiological features [4]. As a result, it opens the multiple catalytic active sites for oxygen adsorption followed by electroreduction; that makes them suitable alternatives to Pt/C as metal-free, low-cost electrocatalysts [5,6]. Carbon based nanocrystal dots (CDs), include carbon nanoparticles of less than 10 nm in size known as carbon quantum dots (CQD) and graphene nanosheets of less than 100 nm in size, known as graphene quantum dots (GQDs) [7–9]. In addition to excellent luminescence, low toxicity and chemical inertness of GQDs, there exists few reports of good ORR performances similar to that of 2D graphene [10–14]. Typically, GQDs contain carboxylic moieties at the edge, similar to graphite oxide, thus making them water soluble and suitable for subsequent functionalization [12]. GQDs also have outstanding characteristics such as high surface area, large diameter and better surface grafting using Π → Π conjugated network [15]. In view of the remarkable properties of CDs, doping them with electron rich groups such as nitrogen (N-CDs) would produce new phenomena and properties by altering the electronic characteristics by increasing the number of active sites [12,16]. Theoretical studies and electronic structural calculations emphasize that chemically bonded nitrogen atoms located at the edge or near the edge of CDs promote electrocatalytic activity of N-CDs towards ORR [17,18]. Further, it was found that co-doping carbon with two heteroatoms (-N, -B or -S) [19–21], can effectively create more catalytically active sites than single element doped counterparts, resulting from synergistic coupling effects between heteroatoms [21]. Also, few more reports have suggested that hetero atom doped carbon nanotubes can catalyze a four electron ORR process with appreciable electrocatalytic activity and better durability than that of commercially available Pt based catalyst in alkaline electrolytes [10,19,22,23]. However, in many of these reports, the catalyst was prepared using expensive methods such as chemical vapour deposition (CVD) and using hazardous materials with high temperature annealing in presence of corrosive gases like ammonia gas. Whereas, recently Liu et al., synthesized a nitrogen doped carbon dots/graphene composites using simple hydrothermal method for efficient ORR catalysis [24]. Beside, the ease of doping CQDs with multiple hetero-atoms by inexpensive one-step chemical methods have gained significant attention due to enhancement of optical, electronic and catalytic properties [14,25]. Thus, such multiple hetero-atoms doped carbon dots could be an appropriate precursor for synthesizing multi-atom doped carbonaceous nanostructures for efficient energy conversion. We also believe that a control over heteroatom content could be achieved using such self-assembly of unique multi-doped carbon dots over other carbonaceous materials such as graphene oxide, nanfibres or carbon nanotubes etc.

Consequently, herewith we have developed a simple hydrothermal method for the synthesis of N and S-doped CNTs (NSCD/C) through a self-assembly of N, S-doped carbon dots on exfoliated multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs). Interestingly, the obtained nanomaterial shows appreciable electrocatalytic activities towards ORR compared to state-of-the-art Pt/C catalyst. Specifically, NSCD/C-600 (N and S-doped CNTs heated 600 °C) catalyst shows Eonset of 0.87 V vs RHE and current density of 5.0 mA cm−2 with a single step nearly 4-electron transfer pathway in alkaline medium. Most importantly, NSCD/C-600 shows better electrochemical stability than that of Pt/C catalyst under similar conditions.

All the chemicals and solvents were used as obtained without any further purification. Anhydrous citric acid (99.5%) and L-cysteine were procured from Fisher scientific and Sisco research laboratories, India, respectively. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and potassium hydroxide (KOH) pallets were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Other solvents like isopropyl alcohol (IPA) and nitric acid (HNO3) were purchased from Rankem chemicals, India. Millipore water was used in all the experiments and electrochemical property measurements.

At the beginning, 100 mg of as received CNT was mixed with concentrated nitric acid in a round bottom flask. The whole mixture was ultrasonicated for 30 min followed by refluxion at 80 °C for 12 h. Finally, the functionalized CNTs were centrifuged and washed with water until the filtrate becomes neutral in pH and was dried in a hot air oven at 60 °C for overnight and designated as fCNT.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon quantum dots (NSCD)
ce:section.ce:para: The nitrogen and sulphur co-doped carbon quantum dots were prepared using a slight modification to the previously reported method [7]. In brief, 2 g citric acid and 1 g L-cysteine were dissolved in 10 mL de-ionized water, followed by slow evaporation at 70 °C until dry syrup within 5 h has appeared. The resulting thick brownish color syrup was transferred to a Teflon-equipped stainless-steel autoclave and heated hydrothermally at 200 °C for 3h. The product which was in the form of dark reddish black syrup that was then diluted to 100 mL after neutralizing it using NaOH solution and was recognized as NSCD. The typical synthesis procedure is described in Scheme 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nitrogen & sulphur co-doped MWCNTs (NSCD/C)
ce:section.ce:para: 50 mg of fCNT was dispersed in aqueous solution of NSCDs using bath ultrasonication (40 kHz) for 30 min to obtain a homogeneous distribution of NSCD over fCNT. The dispersed solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated hydrothermally at 180 °C for 24 h (as shown in Scheme 1). The obtained sample was separated by centrifugation and dried at 60 °C in a hot air oven for overnight and was denoted as NSCD/C. The prepared NSCD/C sample was annealed at various temperatures viz. 400, 500, 600 and 750 °C and was designated as NSCD/C-400, NSCD/C-500, NSCD/C-600 and NSCD/C-750 respectively.

The particle sizes of obtained NSCD sample was determined on Malvern Nano-Series Zeta Sizer (ZS90, Malvern Instrument Ltd., UK) at 25 °C based on dynamic light scattering (DLS) technique. All NSCD/C based samples were characterized using various spectroscopic techniques. Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was recorded on a X’pert pro diffractometer, PANalytical using Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.5406 Å, 40 kV, 40 mA). Raman spectra were recorded on HR 800 Raman spectrometer (Jobin Yvon, Horiba, France) using a He-Ne laser at an excitation wavelength of 630 nm. The photoluminescence (PL) study was carried out on FLUOROLOG®-3 modular spectrofluorometer (HORIBA Scientific) with the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The elemental composition was determined through energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS) using a field emission-scanning electron microscopy (FEI Quanta FEG 200 HR-SEM). The absorption spectra were recorded using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Specord-200 plus, Analytik Jena, Germany). The morphology of prepared sample was studied by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), using FEI Tecnai G2 20 Twin microscope operated at 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopic (XPS) analysis was performed using a Shimadzu ESCA 3400 instrument. All individual spectra were deconvoluted and fitted using Shirley type background function.

The electrochemical studies were carried out on a standard three-electrode cell (CHI760E bi-potentiostat; CH Instruments, Inc., USA) at room temperature. A platinum (Pt) wire and Ag/AgCl (sat. KCl) were used as counter and reference electrode respectively. All the electrochemical measurements were carried out in an aqueous 0.1 M KOH solution. A glassy carbon (GC) electrode, having 3 mm disk diameter was used as a working electrode in the present study. At the beginning, the GC electrode was polished with 0.05 mm alumina powder slurry, followed by careful rinsing using de-ionized water. A uniform catalyst ink was prepared by dispersing 6.0 mg of catalyst powder into solution contains 3 mL of 24 wt% IPA and 20 μL of 5 wt% Nafion, through ultrasonication in ice cold bath for 30 min. Then, 4 μL of catalyst ink was drop-coated onto the pre-cleaned GC electrode. 113.2 μg cm−2 catalysts were then loaded on the GC electrode for ORR test and electrode was dried at room temperature. For comparison, the Pt/C (20 wt%) electrode was prepared using a similar method with actual Pt metal loading of 22.6 μg cm−2. In order to start the measurement, N2 and O2 gas was bubbled at least for 30 min to ensure saturation. Then, this catalyst-loaded working electrode was then electrochemically cleaned by cycling between 0 V to −1.0 V versus Ag/AgCl in an N2 saturated 0.1 M KOH solution. Linear sweep voltammograms (LSVs) were recorded by varying the potential from 0 to −1.0 V with a scan rate of 10 mV s−1 while the rotation was varied from 400 to 2500 rpm. Finally, the durability of NSCD/C-600 catalyst was studied by scanning the working electrode between −0.5 to −0.1 V vs. Ag/AgCl (using a standard protocol of triangular potential vs. time curve with 16 s for a single cycle) at the scan rate 50 mV s−1 up to 5000 durability cycles in O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution. The rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) measurements were carried out by using our previously reported method [26]. At last, the electrode potential was converted with respect to the reversible hydrogen electrode (RHE) [26].

At the beginning, NSCDs were characterized by UV–vis and photoluminescence spectrophotometer. In UV–vis absorption spectra, two dominant peaks at around 243 and 345 nm were observed for NSCDs sample, as clearly seen from Fig. 1 (a). The absorption peak at about 243 nm corresponds to the Π → Π* transition of the sp2 domains of NSCDs, whereas peak at 345 nm was attributed due to the trapping of excited state energy (electrons or holes) by CDs surface [7,15–16,27]. Importantly, there were no peaks due to L-cysteine and citric acid in the similar region. Additionally, the aqueous solution of NSCDs sample shows, a very bright blue luminescence after irradiation of UV light (252 nm) even at a very low concentration (1 μL/mL) [7,12], whereas there was no luminescence under white light (as shown in the inset of Fig. 1(a) and scheme Scheme 1). Furthermore, the emission peak in PL spectrum is a function of the excitation wavelength. Interestingly, the strong fluorescent emission around 415 nm of NSCDs (as shown in Fig. 1b) was attributed due to the presence of surface nitrogen and sulphur atoms [7]. Moreover, the particle size of the prepared nitrogen and sulphur doped carbon dots was determined with Malvern Nano-Series Zeta Sizer using DLS. Fig. S1 (in Supporting information) shows the histogram of particle size distribution for NSCD sample. Here, majority particles have been in the wide diameter range of 100–200 nm due to the aggregation of nanoparticles.

The morphology of resultant NSCD/C-600 catalyst was studied using HR-TEM. Fig. 1(c) reveals a surface defects on CNT sidewalls (as shown by red arrow) thanks to the sonochemical functionalization. As a result, more defects sites were generated on surface of carbon system, which further provides improved three phase boundaries for dioxygen adsorption. Further, the existence of C, O, N and S in NSCD/C based catalyst was confirmed from EDS spectrum, as clearly seen in Fig. 1(d). It is challenging to incorporate more percentage of sulphur content in the resultant CDs due to the atomic size differences as well as the difference in electronegativity between -S and -C (S: 2.58, C: 2.55). Hence there is relatively low doping of sulphur as compared to nitrogen that has been observed in present study [25].

The comparative powder XRD patterns of various NSCD/C samples are shown in Fig. 2 (a), which reveal the intense (002) peak at 2θ = 25.9° value corresponds to the graphitic peak of carbon. Whereas, the weak peak at 2θ = 43.5° value is indexed as (101) plane, which is associated with the defect sites in the carbon phases [26,28]. The peak width varies before and after combination with -N, -S co-doped carbon dots i.e. both the diffraction peaks of (002) and (101) undergo broadening after carbon dots are loaded on CNT. Of course, there is no significant change observed in XRD pattern (crystal structure) of all NSCD/C based samples. However, there are obvious changes on surface of NSCD/C based catalysts (as clearly seen in TEM; Fig. 1(c)), which is key factor for more dioxygen adsorption followed by electroreduction in ORR process. A comparative Raman spectra of functionalized CNTs and NSCD/C-600 sample has been shown in Fig. 2(b), which clearly reveals the presence of both defective (D) and graphitic (G) band at around 1324 cm−1 and 1597 cm−1 respectively. The D-band in resultant nanomaterials can be attributed to the presence of sp3 defective sites in the system and G-band can be assigned to the E2g phonon vibration of sp2 bonded carbon atoms in CNTs [26,29]. A negligible increase in ID/IG ratio of NSCD/C-600 sample (1.63) compared to that in case of fCNT (1.6), indicates slightly higher defect sites in NSCD/C-600 catalyst due to N- and S- doping in CNTs.

The elemental analysis has been carried out using XPS analysis. Accordingly, Fig. 3 illustrates the deconvoluted spectra of C 1s; O 1s; N 1s and S 2p core level for NSCD/C-600 sample. According to Fig. 3(a), C 1s spectra split into four different types of peaks at 284.2, 285.5, 286.5 and 289.57 eV corresponding to CC, CC, CO/CN/CS and CO/OCO respectively, which confirms the N- and S- atoms were doped into the surface of carbon system and it is in good agreement with the previously reported study [14,30]. Further, Fig. 3(b) shows three different types of O 1s peaks with binding energy at 529.8, 532.3 and 534 eV corresponds to carbonyl group (CO), ether linkages (COC) and OCOH functionalities respectively [14,26]. It is important to note that the spectra corresponding to N 1s and S 2p are very weak for their accurate deconvolution. Thus, the deconvolution of N 1s spectrum (shown in Fig. 3(c)) reveals two peaks at 398.5 and 400.3 eV corresponding to CNC and NC linkage, suggesting the presence of trace amount of nitrogen in NSCD/C-600 sample. Similarly, the S 2p spectrum was fitted into two peaks at 167.7 (2p3/2) and 169 eV (2p1/2) that are attributed to sulphur bonded carbon atoms [14].

The electrochemical performance of all NSCD/C catalyst was evaluated in a typical three-electrode cell through cyclic voltammetry (CV) and linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) technique in 0.1 M KOH aqueous electrolyte at room temperature. At the beginning, CVs were recorded for NSCD/C-600 sample in N2 and O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solution within potential range 0 to 1.0 V vs RHE at a sweep rate of 20 mV s−1. As clearly seen in Fig. 4 (a), a well-deﬁned cathodic oxygen electroreduction peak around 0.72 V vs RHE in the O2-saturated condition compared to the N2-saturated condition, demonstrates the electrocatalytic activity of this catalyst towards ORR. To obtain kinetic current information, LSVs were recorded at various rotation speeds at a sweep rate of 10 mV s−1 and in the presence of O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH at 25 °C. The onset potential (Eonset; the potential at which the catalysed ORR current starts to appear) and half wave potential (E1/2) of the ORR curves are used to assess the catalytic activity. Generally, the more positive shift of Eonset indicates the catalyst is most active with lower activation overpotential. It is noted that, among all NSCD/C based electrocatalyst, NSCD/C-600 catalyst exhibits the most positive Eonset of 0.87 V vs. RHE, along with E1/2 of 0.71 V and higher diffusion-limiting current density (JL) of 5.0 mA cm−2 at 1600 rpm, which is clearly seen in Fig. 4(b). Additionally, NSCD/C-600 catalyst follows a Koutecky-Levich (K-L) approach, i.e. current density increases with an increase of rotation speed from 400 to 2500 rpm, hence the kinetics of the ORR was studied by calculating a number of electrons involved in reduction process using Koutechy-Levich equation [31].

C
O 2

In this case, I is measured current, Ik is kinetic current and Id is diffusion-limiting current. The slopes (B) of their best linear ﬁt lines were used to calculate the electron transfer number (n) on the basis of the K-L equation, where F is Faraday constant (96485 C mol−1), A is geometrical area of GC electrode (0.0707 cm2), DO2 is diffusion coefficient of oxygen molecule (1.9 × 10-5 cm2 s−1), v is kinematic viscosity of the electrolyte (0.01 cm2 s−1) and CO2 is analyte concentration (1.2 × 10−6 mol L−1) [26]. Fig. 4(c) shows the K-L plot (J−1 vs. ω−1/2) of NSCD/C-600 sample at various potential, which is obtained from LSVs recorded in Fig. 4(b) and perfect linearity of K-L plots reveal the first order kinetics towards ORR. Moreover, the number of electrons transferred (n) per oxygen molecule was obtained from Eq. 1 and found to be around 3.6 in case of NSCD/C-600 catalyst. In addition to this, RRDE studies give further evidence to K-L approach by noting the amount of peroxide generated at ring electrode during oxygen electroreduction process. Accordingly, inset of Fig. 4(d) shows a inferior ring current (Ir) as compared to the disk current (Id) and the ‘n’ value of the resultant sample is calculated using the RRDE test data and the following equations [32,33].
(4)
% H
O 2 -
= 200 ×
I r
/ N
I d
+
I r
/ N
(5)
n = 4 ×
I d
I d
+
I r
/ N

‘N’ is the current collection efficiency of the Pt ring (in the present case, N = 0.38) determined separately using standard redox couple of ferri/ferrocyanide [26]. Fig. 4(d) clearly reveals that, the NSCD/C-600 sample exhibits peroxide (HO2 −) intermediate yield during ORR is less than 12% and the number of electrons transferred per oxygen molecule was calculated to be 3.8–3.9 over the potential range of 0–0.8 V versus RHE. Generally, commercial Pt/C or Pt based alloy based catalyst shows an ideally single step, 4-electron transfer pathway for oxygen electroreduction. It is observed that NSCD/C-600 catalyst revealing a Pt like first order reaction kinetics during ORR process. Hence, NSCD/C-600 will be considered as one of the promising electrocatalysts to replace noble Pt based catalyst and also to minimise the cost of LTFCs technology.

Fig. 5 (a) reveals the comparative ORR polarization curves of all NSCD/C based catalyst at the constant rotating speed of 1600 rpm under O2-saturated 0.1 M KOH solutions. The increase of Eonset as well as JL with respect to annealing temperatures indicates that, the annealing treatment at various temperatures play a crucial factor for improving ORR activity. Although NSCD/C-750 gives a higher Eonset of 0.88 V & JL of 5.1 mA cm−2 at 1600 rpm, the feature in the voltammogram signifies a mixed two and four electron transfer processes which is unfavourable for ORR. The degradation of catalytic activity and ORR kinetics in case of NSCD/C-750 catalyst may be due to the decomposition of active sites or defects at higher temperature (above 600 °C) due to the reaction between oxygenated functional groups and heteroatoms. Therefore, the NSCD/C-600 is a better candidate compared to other optimized samples. Although the values of Eonset and JL of this sample do not reach state-of-the-art Pt/C catalyst, they are among the remarkable values reported for similar carbon based electrocatalysts [10,11,13,21,34,35]. Thus, the increase in electrode kinetics in case of NSCD/C-600 electrocatalyst is mainly attributed due to the (1) upon optimization of NSCD/C based sample with respect to annealing temperature, which introduces more defect sites in the carbon crystal lattice; and (2) the synergetic effect between both -N and -S co-doped carbon based dots and functionalized CNTs, where the interface can provide ideal situation for dioxygen adsorption followed by electroreduction. Moreover, the Tafel slope further gives the information about reaction kinetics. As shown in Fig. 5(b), NSCD/C-600 electrocatalyst exhibits smaller Tafel slope (89 mV dec−1) compared to other catalysts, signifying better ORR kinetics of NSCD/C-600 sample. These results are in good agreement with our previous discussion on kinetics of ORR for all NSCD/C based catalysts. The activities of all NSCD/C based samples were calculated through the normalization of kinetic current by the total mass of active catalyst at 0.6 V vs RHE. As shown in Fig. 5(c), NSCD/C-600 shows ∼1.7 times higher mass activity (68.4 mA mg−1) than NSCD/C (40.9 mA mg−1). This supports our prediction of temperature effects as well as synergistic effect and hence leading to an increase in a number of active sites such as -C-N- or -C-S-C- for oxygen adsorption. The detailed comparison of activity and other electrochemical property measurements of all NSCD/C electrocatalysts have been listed in Table S1 (in Supporting information). Importantly, upon comparison of electrocatalytic activities of NSCD/C-600 and state-of-the-art Pt/C catalysts with respect to mass activity (68.4 vs 100 mA mg−1) and JL (5.0 vs 5.7 mA cm−2), it is clear that NSCD/C-600 catalyst exhibits comparable electrocatalytic activity with Pt/C (as clearly seen in Figs. 5d and S2). Although, the NSCD/C-600 shows comparatively lower electrocatalytic activity than that of Pt/C catalyst, it exhibits single step ORR kinetics similar to Pt/C.

Finally, the stability and selectivity of NSCD/C-600 electrocatalyst was assessed through electrocycling and chronoamperometric i-t (current vs time) technique and compared with Pt/C catalyst under similar conditions. Interestingly, NSCD/C-600 catalyst exhibits better electrocatalytic stability than commercial Pt/C under alkaline condition. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), NSCD/C-600 catalyst shows very minimal loss (17 mV) in the half wave potential (E1/2) after 5000 durability cycles, whereas Pt/C shows nearly 50 mV loss in E1/2 (as shown in Fig. S2). Thus, NSCD/C-600 exhibits higher stability towards ORR under alkaline condition than Pt/C. Additionally, Fig. 6(b) reveals that the relative current response of NSCD/C-600 sample had less than 20% degradation over 600 s. However, in case of the Pt/C catalyst, about 35% degradation in its current demonstrates a better stability of NSCD/C-600 catalysts than that of Pt/C catalyst under alkaline media. To further evaluate the ORR performance of the NSCD/C-600 under practical conditions, the methanol crossover effects were also examined. Inset of Fig. 6(b) shows that, no appreciable changes in the I vs t curve in case of NSCD/C-600 catalyst after addition of 1 M methanol after 300 s, whereas Pt/C is known to show a very strong response on interaction with methanol. Thus, NSCD/C-600 exhibits higher selectivity for the ORR with better tolerance towards crossover effect than the Pt/C catalyst, which is especially important in direct methanol fuel cells.

In this work, we have designed an N- and S- co-doped metal-free carbon catalyst using simple and inexpensive process. N- and S- co-doped carbon dots introduced defects in the crystal lattice of MWCNTs due to structural distortions. The electrochemical studies reveals that, upon optimization of NSCD/C catalyst showed better ORR kinetics with Eonset of 0.87 V vs RHE and JL of 5.0 mA cm−2 in alkaline condition. Additionally, it shows a single step nearly 4-electron transfer pathway, signifying first order kinetics similar to Pt/C. Importantly, NSCD/C based catalysts showed much better stability as well as selectivity towards ORR kinetics, when compared with Pt/C. The enhancement in the oxygen electroreduction activities is not only due to the structural distortion in carbon system, but also the synergetic effect between both -N, -S and -C atoms and functionalized surfaces of CNTs. Hence, this report provides scope to replace precious noble metal based catalysts with metal-free catalysts such as NSCD/C-600 in cathode compartment for LTFCs applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Department of Science and Technology Science and Engineering Research Board (DST-SERB; No. SB/FT/CS-120/2012), India for financial support through the Young Scientist Fast track scheme. Authors also acknowledge SRM-IST for providing research infrastructure.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apsusc.2017.12.124

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
